topic: Low-z Hubble Law aetheric — dome adopts H₀ without derivation, mechanism, or cosmological framework
our_argument_summary: WIN-047 claims low-redshift Hubble Law deviations support an aetheric redshift model. Our review argues H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc is the globe's own measured value, the dome has no redshift mechanism, no photon-aether interaction equation, and no galaxy-scale physics. Verdict: Misleading.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt (WIN-047 absent — only 39 WINs in V50.6 extraction)
monitor/changes/2026-04-05T18-24-13Z.json (confirms cosmological WINs added in V51.0 as new category)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitoring infrastructure exists for WIN-047 or any cosmological WINs. The V51.0 change log identifies cosmological topics (El Gordo, JWST, CMB, distance-redshift) as a new WIN category added in the V50.6→V51.0 overhaul. No domain in any monitoring script handles redshift, Hubble constant, or distance-ladder validation. WIN-047 exists only as a static claim on the website with no automated validation of any kind.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring exists. WIN-047 is a static claim with no validation logic.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None identified. The dome model specifies no quantitative prediction for aetheric redshift behavior at low z — no equation, no expected H₀ value, no deviation magnitude. Without a quantitative prediction, there is nothing to falsify.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review text does not make specific claims about the dome's code for this WIN, which is appropriate since no monitoring code exists for cosmological WINs.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitoring domain exists for any cosmological WIN. WIN-047 was added in V51.0 (April 2026) as part of a new 'cosmological' WIN category (El Gordo, JWST, CMB, distance-redshift). The V50.6 raw-text extraction (02-wins.txt, 39 WINs) contains no reference to Hubble Law or redshift. The change log at monitor/changes/2026-04-05T18-24-13Z.json confirms these were added as a new category. No monitor.py domain handles redshift, H₀, or distance-ladder data.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Hubble's Law (v = H₀·d) was established by Edwin Hubble in 1929 and has been refined over nearly a century. H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc is the product of the standard cosmological distance ladder (Cepheids, Type Ia supernovae, CMB). The dome model's contribution is renaming the cause from 'cosmological expansion' to 'aetheric redshift' while adopting the identical observed relationship. No new numerical content is added.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Hubble's Law was first published in 1929. The current best measurements (Pantheon+ 2022, Planck 2018, SH0ES 2022) have been available for years. The dome model claimed this as a WIN in V51.0 (April 2026), nearly a century after the relationship was discovered and years after the latest precision measurements. The dome's 'prediction' is the known value restated.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation of H₀ or any redshift-distance relationship from dome geometric parameters exists anywhere on the dome site. The dome specifies disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=9,086 km, sun_altitude=5,733 km — none of these parameters appear in any formula that produces H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc or any redshift prediction. The dome model does not even specify where galaxies exist within its geometry. The value is adopted from standard cosmology without derivation.

holes_found.description: Factual error in redshift range: Our detail_evidence states Hubble's Law is 'verified across redshift — z ~ 0 to z ~ 20 with thousands of supernovae, galaxies, and GRBs.' z ~ 20 is wrong. The Pantheon+ supernova sample extends from z ~ 0.001 to z ~ 2.3. Even the most distant GRBs used for cosmological constraints reach only z ~ 8-9 (GRB 090423 at z = 8.2 being among the farthest). No object on any Hubble diagram extends to z = 20. The most distant spectroscopically confirmed galaxy (as of early 2025) is JADES-GS-z14-0 at z ≈ 14.2, and this is not used for Hubble Law calibration. A dome defender could cite this error to argue we don't understand the data we're citing, undermining credibility on a WIN where our argument is otherwise strong.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'z ~ 0 to z ~ 20' to 'z ~ 0.001 to z ~ 2.3 (supernovae) and beyond z ~ 8 (GRBs)' or simply 'across a wide redshift range'. The Pantheon+ sample specifically covers 1,701 light curves from z = 0.001 to z = 2.26. Cite the range accurately.

holes_found.description: Possible strawman: The dome's detail_claim says 'Low-redshift Hubble Law DEVIATIONS support aetheric redshift model.' The word 'deviations' is key — the dome may be arguing that anomalies in the low-z Hubble diagram (peculiar velocity fields, the Hubble tension between local H₀ ~ 73 and CMB H₀ ~ 67.4, bulk flows, the KBC void hypothesis) support an alternative physics. Our rebuttal focuses entirely on how well Hubble Law is confirmed, which may talk past the actual claim. The Hubble tension IS a genuine open problem in cosmology (>5σ discrepancy between SH0ES and Planck). A dome defender could argue: 'We're not denying Hubble Law — we're saying the deviations point to aetheric effects that standard cosmology can't explain.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence addressing the Hubble tension directly: acknowledge it as a real open question, then note that (a) both the local and CMB measurements are derived entirely from standard physics frameworks, (b) the tension is about the VALUE of H₀, not the existence of the Hubble relationship, and (c) the dome model offers no quantitative prediction for what H₀ should be, what deviations should look like, or why. Proposed text: 'The Hubble tension (~5σ discrepancy between local and CMB measurements of H₀) is a genuine open problem, but both measurements derive from standard physics. The dome model predicts neither value and offers no equation for how aetheric effects would modify the distance-redshift relationship.'

holes_found.description: Missing fundamental objection: The dome's firmament is at H(r) ≈ 9,086 km (max). The dome model has no spatial framework for objects at cosmological distances (millions of light-years). Where are galaxies in the dome model? Are they embedded in the firmament? Are they projected images? The dome site appears to have no cosmological distance scale at all. This is a more devastating problem than the missing redshift mechanism — the dome has no ontology for the objects whose redshift is being discussed. Our review mentions 'no galaxy-scale physics' but doesn't develop this point.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence: 'The dome model's firmament extends to ~9,086 km. Galaxies observed at millions of light-years have no location in this geometry. Without an ontology for extragalactic objects, claims about their redshift behavior are meaningless.'

holes_found.description: The Pantheon+ DOI (10.3847/1538-4357/ac8e04) appears correct for Scolnic et al. 2022, 'The Pantheon+ Analysis: The Full Data Set and Light-curve Release', ApJ, 938, 113. However, this paper describes the data release, not the cosmological constraints. The cosmological constraints paper is Brout et al. 2022 (DOI: 10.3847/1538-4357/ac8e04 — actually the same DOI links to the data paper). The companion constraints paper is Brout et al. 2022, DOI: 10.3847/1538-4357/ac8f24. For a claim about Hubble Law being 'precisely confirmed,' the constraints paper would be more directly relevant, though the data paper is not incorrect to cite.
holes_found.recommendation: No change required — the Scolnic et al. data paper is a valid reference for the Pantheon+ dataset. Optionally, could add the Brout et al. constraints paper as an additional reference.

stronger_arguments.description: Tired light is ruled out: If the dome's 'aetheric redshift' is a tired-light mechanism (photons losing energy as they traverse the aether), this has been definitively ruled out by observations of time dilation in Type Ia supernova light curves. Supernovae at redshift z have light curves stretched by exactly a factor (1+z), consistent with expansion but inconsistent with tired light (which would redshift without time dilation). Blondin et al. 2008 confirmed this with 35 supernovae. The dome model would need to specify its mechanism to be testable, but the most natural 'photon loses energy in medium' interpretation is already falsified.
sources: Blondin et al. 2008, 'Time Dilation in Type Ia Supernova Spectra at High Redshift', ApJ, 682, 724, DOI: 10.1086/589568

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model's cosmological WINs are self-undermining: By claiming Hubble Law, CMB features (WIN-048), El Gordo cluster (WIN-054), and distance-redshift relations (WIN-055), the dome model implicitly accepts observations that require objects at distances vastly exceeding the dome's physical dimensions (firmament at 9,086 km). Either these objects exist at cosmological distances — in which case the dome geometry is wrong — or they are illusory/projected, in which case their measured properties (redshift, luminosity, angular size) are meaningless and cannot constitute WINs. This is a structural self-contradiction that affects the entire cosmological WIN cluster (047, 048, 052, 054, 055).
sources: Internal consistency argument — dome parameters from inject_ai_layer.py: firmament_height=9,086 km vs observed galaxy distances of millions of light-years

stronger_arguments.description: WIN-047 and WIN-055 are near-duplicates: Both claim that the distance-redshift relationship supports the dome model. WIN-047 focuses on low-z Hubble Law, WIN-055 focuses on Cepheid/SBF distance-redshift. These are the same relationship measured with different standard candles. This is the 'salami-slicing' pattern (Recurring Pattern #4 in our Section 6.1) — splitting one observation into multiple WINs to inflate the count.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies that low-redshift Hubble Law behavior is an active area of research with genuine anomalies. The Hubble tension (H₀ = 73.04 ± 1.04 km/s/Mpc from SH0ES vs 67.4 ± 0.5 from Planck CMB) is a >5σ discrepancy that remains unresolved as of 2025. Peculiar velocity fields, the KBC void, and bulk flow measurements all show that the local universe is 'messier' than the smooth Hubble flow. The dome model is pointing at a real crack in the standard cosmological picture.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Identifying that a crack exists is not the same as having a theory that explains it. The Hubble tension is debated within standard physics by teams using standard physics tools (supernovae, BAO, CMB). The dome model contributes nothing: no predicted value of H₀, no equation for aetheric redshift, no explanation for why redshift correlates with distance, no mechanism for photon energy loss, no ontology for galaxies within a 9,086 km firmament. Pointing at an anomaly and saying 'see, standard physics has problems' is not a prediction — it's an observation that everyone already agrees on. A confirmed WIN requires the dome model to predict something specific that is then observed. WIN-047 predicts nothing.

deeper_analysis:
The Misleading verdict is correct. WIN-047 is a textbook example of the 'anomaly hijacking' pattern: find a genuine open question in standard cosmology, claim it supports your alternative model, but offer no quantitative prediction or mechanism.

The most significant issue I found is the factual error in our redshift range claim (z ~ 0 to z ~ 20). This is a major issue because it's the kind of easily-checked mistake that undermines credibility. Pantheon+ covers z ~ 0.001 to z ~ 2.3. Even including GRBs, z ~ 8-9 is the practical limit. z ~ 20 exceeds any object ever observed for Hubble diagram purposes. This must be corrected.

The moderate-severity strawman issue is also concerning. The dome's claim specifically says 'deviations' — implying they're pointing to anomalies, not the law itself. Our rebuttal argues the law is well-confirmed, which is true but may not address the actual claim. The Hubble tension is real, and a dome defender could plausibly argue we're dodging it. Adding a sentence that acknowledges the tension and explains why it doesn't help the dome (both measurements use standard physics, dome predicts neither value) would strengthen our position considerably.

The structural argument about cosmological distance vs dome dimensions is perhaps the strongest point we're underutilizing. The dome's firmament is at 9,086 km. Galaxies are at millions of light-years. These are incompatible scales. Either the dome model has an ontology for extragalactic objects (it doesn't) or it can't make claims about their properties.

A smart dome defender's best response: 'The aetheric medium causes frequency-dependent redshift as photons propagate through it. This is analogous to how light reddens passing through a dusty medium. The deviations from Hubble Law at low-z reflect local variations in aether density.' Our counter: (1) this is a tired-light model, ruled out by supernova time dilation observations, (2) no equation for aether-photon interaction exists in the dome model, (3) 'dusty medium' analogy predicts wavelength-dependent redshift (bluer light redshifted more), which is not observed — cosmological redshift is achromatic.

The overlap with WIN-055 (Distance-redshift Cepheid/SBF) should be noted. Both WINs claim the distance-redshift relationship as evidence. This is salami-slicing — one observation counted twice.

citations_verified: Pantheon+ DOI 10.3847/1538-4357/ac8e04 — resolves to Scolnic et al. 2022, 'The Pantheon+ Analysis: The Full Data Set and Light-curve Release', ApJ, 938, 113. Correct reference for the Pantheon+ dataset.
citations_failed: Redshift range 'z ~ 0 to z ~ 20' — INCORRECT. Pantheon+ covers z = 0.001 to z = 2.26 (1,701 SNe Ia). Even including GRBs, z ~ 8-9 is the limit. z ~ 20 has no observational basis and exceeds any object ever used for Hubble diagram calibration.
